danger_signals.visual_form: Large kaiju-style creature on screen occupying significant visual space
Skyscrapers and stadiums collapsing, buildings falling as combat occurs
danger_signals.meaning: Scale of danger and power being demonstrated through environmental damage

danger_signals.visual_form: Visual damage states on monster bodies, progressive deterioration
danger_signals.meaning: Opponent vulnerability or player advantage in combat state

danger_signals.visual_form: Poison Ghost character composed of toxic waste material
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental or biological threat distinct from physical combat danger

safety_signals.visual_form: Opponent pinned for three count or defeated
safety_signals.meaning: Combat threat neutralized, advancement to next level

safety_signals.visual_form: Astro Guy (Ultraman-like superhero) as playable character
safety_signals.meaning: Heroic protection force available in world

safety_signals.visual_form: Match end via clock running out without pinfall
safety_signals.meaning: Stalemate/draw state indicating temporary safety from loss

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Same monsters appear as opponents but with palette swap (final opponent is palette-swapped version of player's chosen monster)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Monsters are visually scaled to destroy urban environments (skyscrapers, stadiums)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: City-specific architecture and landmarks (Tokyo, Kyoto, Osaka, Kobe, Hiroshima, Okayama)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Same cities appear twice with different visual treatments
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Destructible buildings, stadiums, urban infrastructure serve as stage backdrops

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Modern urban environments with contemporary architecture, skyscrapers, stadiums
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Monsters engage in destruction of futuristic city infrastructure during combat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Six distinct kaiju character designs competing in staged combat across multiple cities
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Monsters progress through tournament bracket structure, fighting each opponent twice in different city locations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Poison Ghost character composed of toxic waste material as threat entity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Toxic entity serves as one of six monster opponents to defeat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Final opponent is palette-swapped version of player's own chosen monster
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must defeat reflection of themselves to prove dominance

environmental_storytelling.example: Monsters kicking over skyscrapers and stadiums during combat visually communicates their colossal size and power relative to human civilization
Game begins and ends in Tokyo while touring other Japanese cities (Kyoto, Okayama, Osaka, Kobe, Hiroshima), suggesting nationwide monster rampage or tournament circuit
Kaiju designs reference both mythological (Moai-like Rocky) and contemporary (Ultraman-like Astro Guy) sources, establishing timeline where both ancient and modern forces coexist

environmental_storytelling.technique: Palette swap mechanic as narrative device
environmental_storytelling.example: Fighting palette-swapped versions of same monsters suggests dimensional cloning or multiplicity of threat, with final opponent being dark mirror of player

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Engages in punch, kick, grab-based combat; destroys urban environment
Uses body slam, pile driver, atomic backdrop attacks; constructed of boulders suggesting durability
Insectoid combat using large creature body for physical attacks
Available as player choice suggesting heroic intervention in monster crisis
Represents environmental/chemical threat distinct from biological kaiju
Progression to next city/opponent; advancement through tournament structure

notes: King of the Monsters operates within kaiju/tokusatsu film aesthetic where visual communication is primarily through creature design and environmental destruction. The world-state is a near-future Japan (1996) under monster threat, conveyed through destructible urban environments and city-by-city progression. Faction identity is tied to individual monster design rather than color or symbol systems. The palette-swap mechanic for final opponents suggests dimensional doubling or dark reflection narrative. Limited faction complexity due to fighting game genre focus on combat mechanics over political systems. Environmental storytelling relies heavily on Japanese urban landmarks and scale indicators (building destruction). No documented visual communication systems for diplomatic/political states; game focuses on combat progression and monster supremacy hierarchy.
